purpose: Declares how important each part of the repo is, as DATA, so a change's required proof is decided by WHICH FILES it touches (a git fact) — not by an LLM's self-rating. Seeded from two independent, blind importance passes, reconciled (docs/proposals/stakes-classification-reconciled.md) under ADR-0006. Owner-legible on purpose: if you can't read this and understand it, it's too complex. Tiers run T0 (most critical) to T4 (noise). A path's tier is the HIGHEST tier whose globs it matches; unmatched paths get default_tier. Escalation rules below can raise a change's tier regardless of path.

T0.name: constitutional / trust root
T0.why: Decides what is true, what an LLM may do, whether work is accepted as proven, whether durable data survives, or whether the safeguards themselves run. A silent defect here is the worst case. Full proof required.

T1.name: integrity-critical runtime
T1.why: Operates important behavior — the app hub, canonical writers, propagation to new projects, the remaining schemas — but is usually downstream of the T0 trust roots. Strong proof required.

T2.name: control-surface & binding policy
T2.why: Shapes behavior and owner decisions — the trust displays, detectors, health report, agent instructions, and binding architecture/policy docs — but is not the final durable or execution authority. Moderate proof.

T4.name: informational / low-consequence
T4.why: Preserves thought or polish but does not determine runtime behavior, canonical state, or acceptance. Near-frictionless by design. (Destructive edits to historical evidence still escalate — see rules.)

escalation_rules.note: These raise a change's tier regardless of the path tiers above. They exist because path alone does not fully determine risk (per the second-opinion review, ADR-0006 / stakes-classification-reconciled.md). Each names a minimum tier it forces.

rules.when: the change deletes or renames any tracked file
rules.why: removal is easy to miss and hard to undo

rules.when: a dependency manifest or lockfile changes
the stakes manifest, the classifier, the commit gate, the receipt writer, the trailer emitter/auditor, the shared identity/receipt-check helpers, or the bypass ledger itself changes
rules.why: the governed entity must never quietly downgrade its own governor

rules.when: CI, git hooks, or the canonical test entrypoint changes
rules.why: changes whether defects are caught at all

rules.when: a test file is deleted (assertion-level weakening of a KEPT test file is NOT auto-detected here — the classifier escalates on deletion only; see its not_proven)
rules.why: removing the proof is as consequential as changing production code

rules.when: a new, unclassified file is added
rules.why: an unknown new path uses the default tier, NEVER the noise tier

rules.when: the change touches more than 25 files OR 3+ top-level areas (app/, scripts/, schemas/, .cockpit/, docs/)
historical/archived evidence is deleted or rewritten rather than appended to

notes.mixed_diff: A change spanning multiple tiers takes the HIGHEST tier among the files it touches (and any escalation).
notes.extensibility: Spawned projects add their own project-specific T0/T1 paths via a per-project extension manifest (ADR-0006); unknown project paths stay at default_tier, never trivial.
notes.granularity: This is FILE-level on purpose: tier is decided by WHICH files a change touches (a git fact), so it cannot be gamed by an LLM SELF-RATING its own change. It is NOT ungameable in general — risky code can be hidden in a low-tier file (path-granularity gaming, accepted residue §6.1). Finer, function-level tiering is deferred until a real miss proves it necessary (e.g. app/main.js is one big file mixing T1 glue with T0-adjacent authority/state code).
